title: Chapter 29: The Root
content: Two months after the Choice, and I'm still learning how slowly real life moves.

No glitches. No splits. No shimmering doorways opening into versions of what could be. Just time, pouring forward like water from a tap I can't adjust, filling the hours whether I'm ready for them or not.
Today the hours are filled with dust.

The café's back room has been a slow-motion disaster since before I started working here — a graveyard of mismatched chairs, broken espresso parts, and boxes that nobody's opened since the Celtic Tiger was still roaring. Siobhán asked me to sort through it last week and I said yes, because saying yes to ordinary things is how I practice staying.

I pull the first box from the stack and cough. Dust billows up, catching the pale light from the single window, and I taste it on my tongue — dry, papery, old. The box is cardboard going soft at the corners, and when I peel back the flaps, I find a jumble of things that aren't café things at all.

Army insignia. Brass and fabric, the kind that pins to a uniform. I turn one over in my fingers — it's warm from the box, or maybe just weighted with something that feels like warmth. A sergeant's chevron. Another badge with a unit number I don't recognize.
Below that: a photograph.

I pick it up by the edges. The texture is soft, almost velvety, the way old photo paper gets after years of being handled and put down and handled again. Six young men in desert gear, squinting against sun that probably felt like standing inside a hairdryer. They've got their arms around each other, grinning with the particular stupidity of people who don't know what's coming yet. Julian C is second from the left. He's younger — not in age so much as in something around the eyes. The parentheses around his mouth haven't been carved yet. He looks like someone who still believes the worst thing that can happen is a flat tire.
And at the bottom of the box, tucked beneath a folded olive-drab t-shirt that still smells faintly of sand and soap — a letter.
The envelope is cream-colored, soft at the creases from being held and reconsidered and put back. A Dublin postmark. Addressed in a careful, rounded hand to Cpl. Julian Blake. Unopened.

I know immediately what it is. Or rather — I know who it's from, because there's only one person who would write to him in that hand, with that care, using the name he had before he became someone else.
I don't open it.
I set the letter on the counter where he'll see it when he comes in for his evening shift, and I go back to the box.

The afternoon stretches. I reorganize what feels like a hundred years of accumulated junk — chipped saucers, a hand-crank blender from the 1970s, three box fans that all need the same wire replaced. Normal problems. Fixable ones. I find a rhythm in it, the way you find a rhythm in chopping vegetables or folding towels. My hands move and my mind goes quiet and for long stretches I don't think about any other version of this moment at all.
He arrives at four, pushing through the kitchen door with his jacket half-off and the wind still in his hair. I hear him pause.
Then nothing.
I come around the corner and he's standing at the counter, the letter in his hands, staring at it like it might bite.
"Mam," he says. Not to me — to the air, or to the envelope, or to the version of himself that didn't open it all those years ago.
I wait.
He turns it over. His thumbs trace the seal — unbroken, all this time. The quiet in the room is enormous.

"I told her I was dead," he says, and his voice has gone thin and careful, the way a wire sounds just before it snaps. "When I ran. I sent word through someone she trusted. Told her Julian Blake died in Cork. Car accident." He swallows. "It was easier than explaining. Easier than letting her hope."

I don't say anything. I don't need to. I've lived three simultaneous lives to avoid difficult conversations. I've invented entire relationships to keep from having real ones. I once told a version of my best friend that I was fine when I was actively bleeding from a decision I hadn't made yet.
I understand choosing the lie that hurts less in the moment. I understand it in my marrow.
He sets the letter down. Picks it up again. Sets it down. His hands are shaking — not badly, just the smallest tremor, the kind you'd miss if you weren't watching for it.
"Can I call her?"

Three words, but they're not what they sound like. Not *should* I. Not *is it wise to*. *Can* I — like he needs someone to grant him permission to want something for himself. Like the wanting itself is a door he's not sure he's allowed to open.
"Do you want to?" I ask.

The silence that follows has teeth. I can hear the refrigerator humming. I can hear a car passing on the street outside, tires on wet asphalt. I can hear his breath, slow and deliberate, like he's counting.
"Yes."
"Then call her."

It's the simplest advice I've ever given anyone. Four syllables. A child could follow it. And it's the hardest advice I've ever had to follow in my own life — this exact principle, this willingness to reach for the thing you want instead of calculating how badly it could hurt. I've glitched across realities to avoid this kind of simplicity. I've split myself into thirds rather than sit with the terrifying arithmetic of wanting.

He nods. Reaches for his phone. Then puts it back down and looks at me — really looks, the way he looked at me two months ago when I told him everything, when I unspooled the whole impossible story of the Fork and the branches and the three worlds I was breaking, and he said he'd spend his life being enough that I wouldn't regret it.
"I'll be in the kitchen," I say.
I go.
From the kitchen doorway, half-hidden behind the espresso machine that needs its gasket replaced, I hear him dial.
One ring. Two. Then:
"Mam."

A pause. I can't hear the other end — just the pitch of a woman's voice rising, climbing, cresting into something I can't quite name. Surprise or grief or both, wrapped so tight together they're the same sound.
"It's me." His voice is steady. Barely. "I know. I know."
Another pause, longer this time. He leans against the counter, one hand gripping the edge like it's the only solid thing in the world.
"I'm sorry."

The word *sorry* comes out cracked down the middle, and I press my hand to my own chest because I can feel it — the pressure of someone breaking open in real time, the sound of a man who's been holding his own death inside him for years finally letting it go.
"I'm in Cork. I'm safe. I have —"
He stops. Breathes.
"I have someone here."
A pause. Whatever his mother says, it makes him close his eyes.
"Yes. Her name is Avery."

And there it is. The first time he's claimed me out loud to someone from his past. Not *a friend* or *someone I'm staying with* or any of the careful half-truths I might have used in his position. *Her name is Avery.* Like I'm his. Like that's a thing he's allowed to say.

Another pause. Longer. I watch his shoulders seize, and then his face does something I've never seen it do — it crumbles, quietly, like the ground giving way under the weight of something that's been standing on it too long.
"I know," he says, and his voice cracks on the second word, splinters it right down to the grain. "I missed you too."
I stand in the kitchen doorway and watch Julian Blake cry for the first time.

Not from pain. I've seen that — on the floor of a place that doesn't exist anymore, in a timeline I chose to end. Not from fear, which I've also seen, in his eyes when the truth of what I was came crashing down on him.

This is different. This is relief — vast and destabilizing, the kind that arrives when you realize the worst thing you imagined wasn't the worst thing at all. The worst thing was living with the lie. Everything after is just weather.

He doesn't try to hide it. He doesn't turn away or wipe his face or apologize. He holds the phone to his ear and cries while his mother talks, and I stay where I am, not moving, not speaking, just being a body in the next room — proof that he doesn't have to be alone in this, even if he's the only one who can do it.
This is what choosing looks like, I think. Not just staying. Coming back.
After closing, we sit on the bench outside the café.

The wood grain is rough under my palms — weathered, salt-stiff, the paint flaking in places where decades of Irish rain have worn it down to the bone of the timber. The bench has been here longer than either of us. It'll be here after. Something about that steadies me.

The sky is doing that thing the Irish sky does when it can't decide what it wants to be — half sunset, half storm, the horizon line lit up like a bruise in gold and violet while clouds stack dark overhead and the air tastes of coming rain. The light makes everything look like a painting that can't commit to a mood. I love it. I love that even the sky here is complicated.
"She wants to meet you," he says.
A statement. And underneath it, a question.
"My mother," he adds, though he doesn't need to.
I lean my head on his shoulder. His jacket smells like coffee grounds and the particular green cold of an Irish evening. Under that, him — warm, alive, here.
"I'd like that," I say.

And the thing is — I mean it. Not in the way I used to mean things, back when I was performing readiness like a party trick, back when I said *I'm fine* or *this is good enough* or *I can make this work* while secretly reaching for the glitch, recalculating, rerouting, keeping one foot out the door of every life I touched.
I mean it the way you mean things when you've stopped leaving yourself emergency exits.

I'd like to meet his mother. I'd like to sit in a kitchen in Dublin and be introduced as *Avery* and answer questions about where I'm from and what I do and have the answers be true. I'd like to be a person who shows up somewhere because she was invited, not because she glitched there by accident.
For the first time, I'm entering a future instead of escaping a present.

The realization lands softly, the way the best ones do — not with a crash, but with a shift so quiet you almost miss it. Like the moment a lock turns. Like the moment you stop holding your breath and don't notice until the air rushes back in.
I check my phone.

It's a normal phone. No Fork app. No shimmering interface, no branching paths, no percentages calculating the odds of my own happiness. Just a screen with a text message and a battery at sixty-three percent.
The text is from Mika.
*Drinks in Dublin next month? I'm finally visiting.*
Three words I never thought I'd love.
I type back: *I'll be there.*
I put the phone face-down on the bench. Julian C's arm tightens around me — a small, automatic gesture, the kind you do when you want to make sure something is still there.

The café's two cats are patrolling the garden, moving along the low stone wall with the proprietary alertness of creatures who have never once questioned their right to occupy space. One of them pauses to glare at a hedge. Business as usual.

Somewhere beyond the fields, the sea is doing what it does — arriving and leaving and arriving again, without strategy, without glitching, without ever wondering if it chose the right tide. It just goes. It just comes back.
The first drops of rain hit the bench — fat, sparse, the kind that can't decide if it's starting or stopping. Neither of us moves.

And Avery Chen — who spent six months living three lives, who fractured herself across realities to avoid a single impossible choice, who held the keys to every door and still, still, still walked through the one that meant giving up the others — is exactly where she chose to be.
Not because it's perfect. Not because it's safe. Because it's *hers*.
Because peace isn't the absence of loss. It's what grows in the space where loss used to live, once you stop running from it.
The rain picks up. We stay.
It's enough. It's more than enough.
It's the root.
